comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Once again, it's technically Monday now -- past midnight Eastern time -- and here I am on Dreamwidth, so it's time for another Magic Monday.  Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all.

With that said, have at it!

***This week's Magic Monday is now closed. See you next week!***

comments.title: On the Four-fold breath in Meditation
comments.content:
From: Lordyburd

Dear Mr. Greer,
I have written to you before on my inability to attain calmness while doing the four-fold breath in preparation for discursive meditation. You advised me to relax and not try too hard. Well, I haven't succeeded too well in that, but I have noticed something new.

I find that I have a sense of a heavy weight somewhere near the bottom of my chest. This makes the out-breath for me easier and relaxing, whereas breathing in and expanding my chest takes effort, and is quite uncomfortable to hold the in-breath for long.

Is this normal?

replies.title: Re: On the Four-fold breath in Meditation
replies.content: It's quite common. That shows you where your deep-seated tensions are located.

comments.title: Sphere of Protection
comments.content:
From: Lordyburd

Dear Mr. Greer,
I am unable to buy (and have shipped) your Druidry Magic Handbook, so I don't know a lot of details about the SOP ritual.

My bedroom, where I perform the SOP, is irregular and has no central space for me to stand. Should I, in order to cleanse this area, go to each side of the room in turn and invoke/banish the elements? That is, go the east side; invoke/banish air, go to the south invoke/banish fire, and so on..till I return to the east again, invoke/banish the Spirits and return to my original position to circulate the light of the cross?
As I circle around the room, should I also trace an imaginary line around it, to roughly define the limits of the Sphere?

replies.title: Re: Sphere of Protection
replies.content: You can find a detailed set of instructions for the Sphere of Protection here. You don't have to have a room with space at the center; just stand wherever you have a little clear space around you, and as you do the working, face east, south, west, north, and east again.

comments.title: Thoughts on Saturday festivities?
comments.content:
St. Patrick’s Day yesterday has me musing. I don’t know if I’ve been under a rock these last few years or if it’s just because the weather in my parts has been early-summer fantastic all this past week, but the level of celebrations this Saturday seemed jubilant to the point of more-so-than-normally disconcerting.

I recall you mentioning how you feel there appear to be many different beings receiving offerings and communicating under the various names of “Jesus”, and I’m now thinking on the relationship of this with those named during other holidays. There was something about the feel of the goings-on this past Saturday that felt a little too cohesively and doggedly sacrificial – like this morning when a group member arrived and stated so matter-of-factly about his hurting head. Yeah, it was _____’s Day - what else was he going to do? What I saw of the young people around when wandering the neighbourhood yesterday seemed a little like folks were practically throwing themselves at the nearest urinal (yeah, mixing metaphors) saying “let me feed you!”.

There may be similarities between this past Saturday and other equinox-ish festivities with lots of green and bright colours, but I wonder if you feel any folks with whom humans have better long-standing relationships are likely to benefit from this activity, given the seeming lack of social structure or guidance beyond straight go-for-broke excess? I may be straying right into "projecting the shadow" territory here but it's more the lack of direction combined with the magnitude of the activity that has me worried more than anything else!

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Saturday festivities?
replies.content: Interesting. To use Jungian language, this is the way things look when an archetype is in the saddle -- you can replace that word "archetype" with "spirit" or "god" and the old wizard from Zurich wouldn't have batted an eye. The question in my mind is purely just how sacrificial it's going to get.

comments.title: On invoking the Elements
comments.content:
From: Lordyburd

I also have a general query regarding asking assistance from the elements. I usually ask the elements to help me with general things, long-term things,almost like a prayer. For example: mental clarity and alertness from Air, Courage from Fire, Mastery of my emotions from Water, Financial independence from Earth and Personal and Spiritual Mastery from the Spirits. Things have improved quite a lot over the course of the past year, but the process is slow and times non-discernible. I wonder if this so because I don't usually ask for anything specific, as often a specific goal seems contradictory to some other aspect of my life that I want to change?

replies.title: Re: On invoking the Elements
replies.content: To my mind, you're doing things in exactly the right way. It's always at least a little risky to ask for specifics, since you have no way of being sure that you really do need the specific thing you think you need! Focusing on broader goals is slower, yes, but the results are surer. Stick to it, and you can expect things to just keep on improving for you.

comments.title: Tower of Light Exercise
comments.content:
Hello John.

I have started practicing an exercise called The Tower of Light from a book called The Practical Guide to Psychic Self-Defense by Melita Denning and Osborne Phillips.

In case you're not familiar, it's basically a standing meditation/ritual where you begin by visualizing a long ellipsoid of bright blue light nine to sixteen inches around your body. Then, you visualize a brilliant sphere of white light above your head, which represents your higher self. Finally, you visualize the sphere of white light above your head sending down glittering white light, flooding your aura and at the same time coursing vibrantly through you.

My questions for you are:

1. Does this seem like a safe exercise for a complete beginner?
2. Could this be used to replace a daily banishing ritual? I cannot help it, but I like this exercise better than any of the banishing rituals I've ever encountered.

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Tower of Light Exercise
replies.content: Yes, it's suitable for beginners, and if that's what draws you, by all means. You may want to read some of Denning and Phillips' other books -- they taught a complete system of magic, the Aurum Solis system, and your attraction to the Tower of Light may be a sign that the broader Aurum Solis system may be well suited to your needs further down the road.

comments.title: Regarding communicating with disembodied entities
comments.content:
From: Lordyburd

Forgive me, Mr. Greer; one more question. A few weeks ago I asked about contacting an entity that helped me during one of my periods of mental turmoil. It seemed to appear in my imagination as a 'wooden cat'. Upon asking, you recommended I calmly try to visualise this image and attempt to ask its name and nature.

I find that I am unable to deliberately bring up a clear and stable image of this. I just keep getting continuously changing outlines of figures against a black silhouette. Perhaps I am trying too hard? Or is it that consciously imagining for a specific intent is a skill that comes with time and practice?

replies.title: Re: Regarding communicating with disembodied entities
replies.content: In that case, it may not be time for you to pursue the contact further. Thank the entity and let it know that you're open to conversation at a later time, and then let the matter rest.

replies.title: Re: Regarding communicating with disembodied entities
replies.content: Thank you Mr. Greer!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
When you were practising radionics what kind of results did you get? With the more technological means of self-improvement like mind machines that use light, sound and electromagnetism to alter the brain, are their effects just material or do they extend to other planes as well?

In your Galabes post on occult philosophy you said higher level Theosophical teaching could react badly with operative magic. How would that clash happen and what other comibinations of occult philosophy, other spiritual practices, and magic are known to go wrong? Is this the same way Tai Chi and Golden Dawn cause problems when combined or is it something different?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I used a homebuilt transistorized Hieronymus machine -- I still have it, along with all the relevant manuals, in case I get a bug up and decide to get into that again. It doesn't use light, sound, or electromagnetism; T. Galen Hieronymus, the inventor, was one of a long line of Western researchers to discover the life force independently, and that's what it used. (Mesmer called it "animal magnetism," von Reichenbach called it "odic force," Hieronymus called it "eloptic energy" -- it's all our old friend the life force, aka qi, ki, prana, vril, nwyfre, or (ahem) voor.) Its effects aren't material at all, they're astral and etheric; in my experience, in fact, a Hieronymus machine is a tunable talisman, capable of radiating magical influences of any kind you like, provided you know the correct vibratory rates at which to set the dials.

As for operative magic and the inner teachings of Theosophy, what I know is purely that the Theosophical societies strictly forbid members of their Esoteric Sections from practicing operative magic or belonging to other initiatory organizations, Freemasonry alone excepted. I assume they know what they're doing, and prohibit that for a reason. In the same way, if you're studying a system of esoteric training, whatever it might be, and your teacher or your textbook says "don't do this," don't argue.

comments.title: The Nine Realms of Midgard
comments.content: I know it's not exactly your field, but I wonder if you're at all familiar with Edred Thorsson (Steven Flowers), his Rune-Gild, or his book/runic curriculum The Nine Realms of Midgard. I've been doing rune-work for some time, principally through divination and through meditation on the rune-poems, but haven't yet worked through a practical magical course on them—and I've been feeling the pull to go deeper into their mysteries. However, I'm unfamiliar with the author and his coursework, and I haven't yet heard from anybody else who's worked through it to see if it tends to manifest any nasty side-effects (such as lives-resembling-smoking-craters), or if the Gild/Thorsson tend toward any of the uglier or more abusive behaviors one occasionally runs into in occult or heathen groups. I'm not necessarily suspicious of any, but some preemptive research seems like a good idea. (Since I know Ásatrú isn't your thing as such, I'm also tossing this question out to the readership, if anyone is inclined to answer.)

replies.title: Re: The Nine Realms of Midgard
replies.content:
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stephen_Flowers
Quite the controversial figure, so have a BIG shaker of salt next to you as you read.

replies.title: Re: The Nine Realms of Midgard
replies.content: I'm only very slightly familiar with Dr. Flowers. Since that entire end of the occult scene doesn't attract me at all, I haven't looked into his work, and don't have anything like the knowledge necessary to offer you useful advice.

replies.title: Re: The Nine Realms of Midgard
replies.content:
I have, and have read, and value, most of the publications of Edred Thorsson (who is Stephen E. Flowers in mundane life). He's a fine scholar who has mastered the academic literature pertaining to the runes, and is (in my judgement) quite sound on the subject. His doctoral dissertation is titled _Runes and Magic_, and it is an excellent account of all the attested magical uses of runic writing. (Runes began as just another alphabet for everyday use; but as with any alphabet, there were obvious magical applications, and the ancient peoples who used runes swiftly developed magical uses for them, too.) This was, and still is, an extremely unpopular position among runologists, most of whom are terrified of any association, however remote, with the loathed rune magicians and esotericists connected with Heinrich Himmler's SS.

He is also excellent on the history of modern rune magic and many of the esoteric movements in the Germanic world that took their inspiration from the runes and from the Germanic pre-Christian (Heathen) past.

He is very knowledgeable in the history and practices of what is usually called the Left-Hand Path. This includes BDSM magic, which (to judge by his rather frank book on the subject) seems to be a part of his and his wife's own personal magical practice. He was for a long time (and perhaps still is) a high-ranked member of the Temple of Set, headed by Dr. Michael Aquino.

As for the magical system recommended in his _Nine Doors of Midgard_, I haven't studied it closely enough to form an opinion as to its value.

He is currently in the process of reconstructing an even older form of Indo-European religion than Germanic Heathendom, which he calls "The Mazdan Way."

Back in the days when Flowers was a new PhD, in the running for a university position in runic and Germanic studies, he was the target of a deliberate smear campaign by people who favored other, more mainstream applicants; these people deliberately "outed" him, made it well known that he was also the occultist Edred Thorsson, and a member of the Temple of Set--which at that time was still widely confused with Anton Szandor LaVey's notorious Church of Satan. Basically, they smeared him so vilely as to make it impossible for any university with any sense of PR to hire him for any prominent academic position, no matter how strong his qualifications and how excellent his scholarship might be. IMHO, he made a silk purse out of the sow's ears he was stuck with, and went on to somewhat better things than the academic world could ever have offered him. But he is still one of the more controversial figures in all the modern North American occult and magical scene. People who value any of his work (as I do) can expect a certain amount of blowback for that alone.

And, to address directly the most common smear against him, I have found no evidence in any of his writings that he holds racist or White nationalist views of any sort. That said, he is very clearly not a "Progressive" or a party-line "Leftist" either. He appears to me, from his writings, to be wholly apolitical as to all of the most divisive issues of our time. But I am not acquainted with him personally; I know him only through his very many writings, and to a small extent from interactions with people who are acquainted with him.

-- Robert Mathiesen

replies.title: Re: The Nine Realms of Midgard
replies.content: Back when I was considering an academic career, I heard about what happened to Flowers, and I also got to talk, via a mutual friend, with another young scholar with excellent qualifications whose work strayed too close to occultism, and who ended up having to take a job teaching English in Inner Mongolia. (I swear I am not making this up!) I looked at what I wanted to study and write, and for better or worse, waved goodbye to academia. So I owe him that much. ;-)

replies.title: Re: The Nine Realms of Midgard
replies.content: Thanks—I find that tremendously informative, and even more more reassuring. Preliminary skimming through the book teased a lot of really rich interesting material, but a couple items listed in the bibliography (mainly pertaining to Nazi esotericism) had me a bit leery.

comments.title: Post ceremony
comments.content:
Hi John Michael,

I hope you eventually get some sleep. It is early evening here and I am in an ebullient mood from taking the absolute mickey out of international diplomacy in this weeks blog! It deserved the good kicking it got too. Writing can sometimes be tiresome and the words have to be dragged from your carcass kicking and screaming all the way. And other times it is a true joy.

Apologies, again I digress, it is an unfortunate habit of mine.

I was curious as to your state of feelings after a ceremony. I tend to feel mildly buoyed, curiously centred and calm. What is your experience? I often feel the same way after a days hard labour and I get a lot of my discursive meditation through that mode, although it is probably unconventional for most people.

Has it warmed up yet? It is so dry here, that there have been some large bushfires in the south west of this state and along the coast of the state to the north of here. Such is global wierding because it is very late in the season to be confronted by that environmental threat.

Chris

replies.title: Re: Post ceremony
replies.content:
It depends very much on the ceremony. Seasonal celebrations and basic practices such as banishing rituals tend to leave roughly the same condition you've described; other workings have different effects -- when I do practical magic for some specific purpose, I tend to end up feeling the kind of pleasant tiredness that comes after a long day of productive work!

No, it hasn't warmed up yet. We're expecting snow again Wednesday.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
What are your thoughts on a binding spell to sever a relationship? There's someone I used to be friends with who I had a major falling out with, and have given far too many chances. She refuses to go away, and every so often tries to get back into my life, and when I say something along the lines of "I gave you several chances, and you wasted all of them," the response is a temper tantrum, which generally includes a lot of rumors being spread about me.

I've tried everything else I can think of, and now I'm left with only one idea: binding us to never have any effect on each others' lives. I know it means I can never affect her life, which is fine with me. Are there any risks I need to know? I'll meditate on it, and use divination before casting the spell, but is there anything in particular I should keep in mind?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: What you want is called a "cut and clear" spell; there are lots of them out there, and they're usually pretty safe to do, because the goal is to cut the connection both ways, so she goes her way and you go yours. Yes, you can combine a binding with that, and a little hotfoot powder doesn't hurt either. ;-)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I've been looking at them, and it seems like a lot are intended to change my views on things, which isn't what I'm looking for. Do you know of somewhere that I could look that would provide either a spell or sources that might lead to one?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Here's a good source from the conjure tradition:

http://www.luckymojo.com/cutandclear.html

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you. This looks like exactly what I'm looking for.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Keeping up with the daily practices of the druidry handbook:

1-I no longer have problems with mental chatter while meditating.On the other hand,many insights and thinking paths about the themes come to me after the meditation itself, on the next day while tending the garden, or cooking etc.What is your opinion on that?

2-One of those ideas i would like to hear your take. It was on the theme"The hidden spirit... That creates all things" If the natural fenomena contain spirit in the form of elementals, what about the human situations? Wars,Religions,Revolutions,but also Marriages, rivalities, murder, associations...?

3- While im arranging to craft Ogham cards, im divinating with geomancy.My wife, who loves tarot, find geo bare and dry.It certainly looks that way compared to the colorfull marseillese tarot, but it ocurred to me that geo forces you to rely more and thus to train intuition and other subtler capacities.Would you agree?

Many thanks,

Guillem

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) That's very common. What's happening is that your meditation is setting the deep levels of your mind working, and ideas surface from it thereafter.

2) Good. Yes, exactly. Everything in the universe is the product of spiritual influences.

3) Different people find different forms of divination appealing. I can't get runes to work for me at all, but geomancy has always worked well for me; I know people who get excellent results with the runes and can't use geomancy at all. The same is true for every other divination system. If your wife finds that tarot works best for her, then she should keep on using tarot; if you get good results from geomancy, and find that it helps you develop your intuition, then do that thing. There is no one right way for everyone!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thanks for your reply.

About spirit, i guess your answer could be my meditation theme tonight :)

PD: The vague response was intentional right?

Best regards,

Guillem

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Of course. As a mage in training, you'll benefit from working out the fine details yourself.

comments.title: Elites and magic
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
Not a technical question, but more one concerned with magical practices.
Throughout history, leaders have either practiced themselves,or engaged the services of practitioners.
Do you think this is still happening to any great extent nowadays?
My feeling is yes, why wouldn't they?

Also, with Saturn now in its home sign of Capricorn (that'd be my sun, and AC by the way), do you think there will be a few (more) comeuppances to be had?

Here in Oz we are currently in the first weeks​ of what has been scheduled for a year long Royal Commission into the banking and finance sectors.
There has already been a bit of dirty laundry aired.
Regards, Helen

replies.title: Re: Elites and magic
replies.content:
"Throughout history" is a little too broad. In societies that accept magic, leaders use magic. In societies that don't, they generally don't. Here in the US, if the leadership of our two main political parties had the first clue about the laws of magic, or listened to competent practitioners, they wouldn't do anything like as many stupid things.

Saturn is now in his rulership in Capricorn, and Mars is in his exaltation in the same sign! Expect to see hard limits come into play, and a lot of hard and productive work ahead.

comments.title: Magic and politics
comments.content:
I just got back from a trip, so I have to go and look over your other posts on writing, but I just wanted to ask this question.

I seem to recall you saying something one time akin to: "politics is where religion goes to die."

Did you say/write something according to these lines, or am I misremembering?

One of the groups I am a part of has been posting more and more political screeds/rants, and while I could ignore them when they were infrequent, and I enjoyed working with the group. But now, they recently have invoked certain Irish deities to make their point in regards to the historic plight of Irish and Irish-Americans, and now how Irish Americans seem to ignore the suffering plights of others today and seem to be Trump supporters.

My main druid/pagan group, however, has strict guidelines on no political talk within the group, and it has worked out wonderfully, knowing that the group holds a wide variety of opinions, but when we do gather together, it is to celebrate the holy days and praise our Gods. But I'm seriously considering breaking ties with the former group.

replies.title: Re: Magic and politics
replies.content: Yes, I did say that, and I'll say it again: when politics and religion get together, politics cuts religion's throat and dresses up in religion's stolen garments. The separation of church and state (or, ahem, coven and state) is there to protect the churches and the covens to a much greater extent than it's there to protect the state! In your place, I'd walk away from the former group sooner rather than later.

replies.title: Re: Magic and politics
replies.content: Why do you think this is? Is it just because people tend to be strongly motivated by politics, or do you think there's more to it?

replies.title: Re: Magic and politics
replies.content:
I’m no expert, but here’s my take on it:

Religion is strongly associated with morality (at least in our Judeo Christian traditions) while politics has ample power and often has a lot of corruption. Political theater likes to dress up its actions in religious/moral terms to hide the corruption and to claim legitimacy for its power. It has the added bonus of mobilizing lots of religious supporters. Political supporters tend to be a lot more devoted when morality is at stake.

Religion develops the same problems when it is also a political power in its own right (like the Roman Catholic church in the Dark Ages right before Martin Luther showed up with a pamphlet, a hammer, and a nail).

Jessi Thompson
anotheramethyst

replies.title: Re: Magic and politics
replies.content: I suspect there's more to it than that, but I haven't yet worked out an explanation that makes sense to me.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
A number of people know I'm into the occult. As a result, I've been given access to several workings aimed to disrupt Trump's administration, often by people who otherwise have no use for the occult. They assume that since I'm a decent person I'm on their side of the political debate, and just assume I'll help.

Except, I'm a Trump supporter.

So I'm sitting on several different rituals that people are casting to try to bind, disrupt, injure, kill, or remove Trump from office, as well as kill, injure, silence, or convert his followers. I've put together various ways to counter them, both as a means of throwing a monkey wrench into plans people have that don't align with my own, and as a form of self defense. If someone casts a working to try to kill me I'm going to make sure it won't work.

I have two observations here: one, these workings are just awful. They're being cast by people who don't know what they're doing, and the intentions are ones that, if they work, are very likely to blow up in their faces.

The second is that this utter inability to grasp that thinking people can disagree with them is going to be the death of the left. They are sharing these workings with people who don't want what they do. I've been gentle, just disrupting the spells. Someone else I know found a hole to exploit: the working includes the phrase "and may all who ever support him die"; and intends to amplify the blow-back from that, such that anyone who casts that working, who ever supports Trump on anything, dies.

I wonder now how much of the utter incompetence of the democrats right now is because they're casting ill thought out spells and showing them to the world...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
"...and may all who ever support him die."

Ahem.

These are the self-proclaimed "good people," the people who claim to be compassionate and caring and enlightened and just, and they're summoning demons and casting death spells on their political opponents. Just who is acting like Lord Voldemort here?

If this continues, the American left will not survive. I mean that quite literally. Political movements sometimes implode, leaving nothing behind but bad memories and scattered circles of embittered has-beens.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I also think the blowback from these workings may very well shorten a few of their lives too....

comments.title: Breathing presence
comments.content:
Hi JMG - thanks for the opportunity to ask questions here.

I'm working through Learning Ritual Magic. In the last week, during meditation I heard deep regular breathing in the studio where I practice. It was so loud and so something, I got up to check the dog hadn't snuck in or for any other explanation. Continuing meditation, I stayed with it as a sound/ presence and noticed it stopped after the first Q Cross, but returned later in the ritual. I've also notice cicadas stop at same time as I start ritual. These two things seem to be sound/ vibration related. comments.title: Multiple Practices

replies.title: Re: Multiple Practices
replies.content: I would not do this. Choose one or the other, do it with all your heart, and then after you've learned as much from the one path as you can, take up the other and pursue it with the same focus. You'll get better results.

replies.title: Re: Multiple Practices

replies.title: Re: Multiple Practices
replies.content: One at a time is your best rule with any spiritual path. You'll get furthest if you give each path your whole heart.

replies.title: Re: SoP
replies.content: Yes, I studied angel lore pretty systematically when I was training in classic Golden Dawn magic -- I found that rituals invoking angels had all the advantages of Goetic summonings and none of the downsides. The archangels you want in that case are Metatron for Spirit Above and Sandalphon for Spirit Below.

replies.title: Re: SoP

replies.title: Re: SoP
replies.content: Start with Gustav Davidson's A Dictionary of Angels and then follow up with the sources listed in his bibliography.

comments.title: A few CGD Questions
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I'm doing the Celtic Golden Dawn path and am wondering about Abred and the Etheric/astral plane. Perhaps I'm getting hermetic and Druidry practice confused but I'm wondering where emotions and then imagination and then abstract thought fit into the tree. My understanding is that my emotions (e.g. lust) are one level, and then I have feelings about my feelings (eg guilt) and then I have imagination (eg fantasy) and the I have concentrated thought (eg focused meditation). What realms am I in when engaging with these? I'm asking partly to see how i might use the practices to help me get clarity in some of my relationships.

Or do you have other suggestions to help with that question?

I'm using divination too, but still learning about it - particularly with what questions to ask - what kind? how specific? Can you recommend something to help with those?

And when I'm imaging my body filling with light, is that imagination or higher level thinking? My light body seems to have less "brilliance" over the months since I started practising and I'm wondering why that may be?

Many thanks!

replies.title: Re: A few CGD Questions
replies.content:
Okay, lust is a passion, not an emotion. On the Tree of Life, lust corresponds to Ner (Yesod), while guilt, which is an emotion, manifests in Byw (Netzach). Thought in the sense of mental chatter belongs to Byth (Hod), while thought in the sense of a reflection of universal patterns in the mind is Muner (Tiphareth); imagination is simply thought using images rather than verbal concepts, so the same rule applies.

As far as getting clarity in your relationships, meditation's a good tool there, and I also recommend a variety of psychology called Transactional Analysis, which you can learn quite adequately from books; Eric Berne's Games People Play is a good introduction.

In the early stages of divination training, start with simple readings and questions such as "What do I most need to understand about the events I experience this day?" As for the apparent decrease in brilliance, that's common, and it usually means you're starting to perceive some of the grubby energies you've picked up over the years; keep practicing and it'll clear up in due time.

replies.title: Re: A few CGD Questions
replies.content:
That is uncanny, I picked up Games people play a couple of weeks ago. And just got hold of another TA book, so i will definitely read them.

Thanks for the distinctions on differnt kinds of thought too, although I'm not clear on the difference between a passion and an emotion. Where might I get more info on that?

Thanks for the other insights too, I'm enjoying the divination which is so "not me" - whatever that means!
Your a star, thank you.

comments.title: Self initiation vows
comments.content:
How much is the intention of an initiation vow dependent upon what the person making the vow perceives as good? For example, I'm interested in the Celtic Golden Dawn, but part of the initiation vows are something like, "I will not use magic for selfish ends..."

I don't want to use magic to gain unfair advantages over others, but at the same time, it seems fine to me to use it for self-serving reasons (improving your salary, etc) if you aren't trying to hurt people.

I guess I'm worrying about the results of taking the vow, and the chance that I might accidentally do something that would unintentionally break it.

replies.title: Re: Self initiation vows
replies.content:
Excellent! It's very good that you're taking prospective vows of initiation seriously. The Ovate initiation includes a pledge never to use magic for selfish or destructive ends, and if you're going to take that pledge, it's wise to be sure that you mean it.

What does the word "selfish" mean? In common usage, which is good enough for our purposes, it means doing things that benefit you even when they harm others. Getting a promotion so that your salary goes up isn't selfish; getting more money by taking it away from other people is.

The point of such clauses is to make you ask yourself, before you decide on any magical operation, "is this selfish? Is this destructive?" -- and if it is, to do something else instead. Remember that in formulating a magical intention, you can always include a phrase such as "in such a way that no one is harmed," or what have you -- and by and large, such things are wise to include in your intentions.

comments.title: Sphere of Protection
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Once again, many thanks for hosting these ask-me-anything posts. Not only have you been very generous with your time in answering my questions, reading your responses to questions from others has been really educational. Much appreciated!

I have a couple of questions about the Sphere of Protection ritual that I don't believe I have seen before:

First, my routine has been to do the SOP at night. I am wondering what the implications on the effectiveness of the ritual are when I go to sleep soon afterwards, besides better dreams and sleeping. What I am getting at is that it intuitively seems like it would be better to start the day with the SOP, and I gather that is what most of the other practitioners are doing. Any thoughts on this?

Second, it surprises me that you refer to the SOP as a banishing ritual, and that banishing rituals are one of the core practices in occult training. I think of the SOP more as a blessing ritual. Can you elaborate on the particular importance of the banishing aspect, and why that is so important?

Samurai_47

replies.title: Re: Sphere of Protection
replies.content:
Some people do the SoP in the morning, some do it at night. I prefer to do my rituals first thing in the morning, but your magical mileage may vary.

As for the SoP as a banishing ritual, it includes a banishing component, and thus fills the same slot in magical training as a more straightforward banishing such as the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram. It's crucial to have something in your training routine that chases off hostile and unbalanced energies and entities -- the lack of that leads quite reliably to certain overfamiliar pathologies of magical training -- and in my experience, the SoP does it quite adequately. It's simply a somewhat more complex practice, that has aspects other than banishing.

comments.title: Aries Ingress Chart
comments.content:
Hi JMG, may I ask are you still planning on analyzing the Aries ingress chart? Also, if I may, I've been spending some time looking at it and would like to run my thoughts by you and others who are more experienced in the arts of Astrology than I. To those reading this, if there is anything I'm getting totally wrong please let me know!

The Aries Ingress chart places the Sun in the 10th House in Aries and the Moon in the 11th in Taurus, Cancer rising. Since Cancer is a cardinal sign the chart will last for three months. The Sun represents the President and the Moon represents the people, especially the more politically vocal people. According to http://www.skyscript.co.uk/ingresses.html the 10th house is the house of the government, while the 11th is the house of legislative bodies. Joining the Sun in the 10th house are Mercury and Venus perfectly conjunct and Neptune. This would mean, I would wager, that the media and the popular arts will be siding with the administration in the three months ahead. Neptune in the 10th would mean that the dreams of the people are tied up with the administration. Joining the moon in the 11th house is Uranus who brings with him political tension to congress to say the least. The Moon is sextile the Neptune, indicating that the people can more easily utilize the American Dream than the administration.

So far so good, it is interesting to note here that the Sun and the Moon have, to my reckoning, the same essential dignity both being in their triplicity.

Now comes the interesting part: Saturn is in the 7th house, in Capricorn. This is a more essentially dignified Saturn than either the Sun or Moon. Saturn in mundane astrology signifies public sorrow. The 7th house represents foreign affairs and is the house of open enemies, and Saturn the Greater Malefic is not a welcome figure here. Even worse, conjunct with Saturn in the 6th house is Mars, representing the Military. Amongst other things the 6th house represents the Armed Forces. Since Saturn and Mars are conjunct we can expect sorrow coming from a foreign power which a military response

Saturn and Mars are significantly trine the Moon but square the Sun! This would indicate that the foreign power share the same interests as the intellectual elites but has interests opposed to the administration. Mars is trine Uranus indicating that the military will effortlessly take part in the political tensions on the side of congress. It should be noted that Mars in the 6th house is peregrine and thus weak. Since it would represent US armed forces, this would mean that US troops would likely be in a correspondingly weak position.

Jupiter, retrograde and peregrine in Scorpio, is also in a very very weak position. Jupiter here signifies the wealth holders of the nation. Jupiter is trines with the Sun and Neptune, indicating that the wealthy and powerful side with the "American dream" and the administration. Again it should be noted that Jupiter is in a very weak position, so it can aid the Sun and Neptune but is an uncertain ally.

I know you don't use Pluto, but I also find it compelling that Pluto is in the 7th house of this chart, squaring the conjunct Venus and Mercury and also squaring Uranus and sextile Jupiter. Pluto being the asteroid of secrets in the house of open enemies would imply perhaps the unseen subversion of political unrest with Uranus, undermining the Media and Popular art and working in the interest of the wealth holders who are, perhaps siding with unknown hostile foreign elements.

Mercury and Venus are in very week positions; Venus is in her detriment and peregrine. Mercury is in his term and is conjunct Venus so is doing a little better. Nonetheless they are besieged by Mars. They are also under the Sun's beams (within 17 degrees of the Sun) which weakens them further. Thus it is fair to say that they won't be able to shift public opinion very strongly.

Altogether, it appears that foreign powers will be playing the administration against public opinion/congress. If I may be so bold, I think that this chart implies the sort of foreign funded unrest you have suggested for awhile JMG, with Saturn working a "divide and conquer" strategy. And a weak US military that is under Saturn's sway, perhaps reacting blindly to the well positioned enemy's initiatives. Altogether, with the accidental dignities, it appears that the Sun is in a significantly weaker position than the Moon. Given that the rising sign is in Cancer I imagine the actors behaving secretively, emotionally and delicately under a spiny shell. Looks like we're in for a very 'interesting' three months!

replies.title: Re: Aries Ingress Chart
replies.content: Good. I'll be posting my delineation the fourth Wednesday of this month on the blog, so we can compare notes at that time.

comments.title: Writing About Magic
comments.content:
Hi, JMG! As always, thanks for doing this.

My question ties in with the writing project you're leading. I'm wondering if you have any thoughts about magic as it's used in fiction.

As I've learned more about ritual magic (mostly from you and your books) I've had a growing urge to include mage characters in my novels/stories.

My main concern is offending people. When I write these characters, my use of magic isn't always faithful to reality, and it goes without saying that I'm not exactly a wealth of knowledge on the topic just yet.

What recommendations would you offer a writer that's a fan (maybe not the best word-choice, but I think you get my meaning) of ritual magic, who wants to avoid writing something that cheapens the practice or offends the practitioners?

replies.title: Re: Writing About Magic
replies.content:
There's so much incredibly bad magic in fantasy fiction these days that literally anything you do that derives from real magic will be a huge improvement. I'd love to see more fantasy that had people using real magic -- and even if you stretch things a bit, why, so do I in my fantasy novels.

My recommendations? Read a lot about magic, especially from books that are written by practitioners. Consider borrowing spells directly from old sources. If you do that, always change the words and other details of the spells you borrow -- there are always clueless kids who try to perform rituals straight out of their favorite fantasy novels, and so it's good practice not to give them anything real. Other than that, have fun, and remember that a lot of mages are into fantasy fiction, and will cut you slack.

Oh, one other thing: a lot of old fantasy has much better magic than the current stuff. The scene in C.S. Lewis' novel That Hideous Strength in which Ransom and Merlin summon the oyarses of the planets is, for my money, the best description of an evocation of planetary intelligences in print. E.R. Eddison did an equally bang-up job, though with more obviously fantastic elements, of the conjuring of demons by King Gorice and Lord Gro in The Worm Ouroboros. Fletcher Pratt's two fine fantasy novels, The Well of the Unicorn and The Blue Star, are worth reading for their elegant treatment of magic as for many other reasons -- and of course there's William Morris, who invented modern fantasy fiction, whose
mighty novel The Well at the World's End some years ago supplanted The Lord of the Rings as my favorite fantasy novel ever, and whose handling of magic is a model modern writers could well learn from. Read and study these, and you'll learn a lot.

replies.title: Re: Writing About Magic
replies.content: This is exactly what I was looking for! Thank you. And thanks for the recommendations.

comments.title: Which Stag?
comments.content:
Good morning JMG,

My question is about something that recently occurred during my discursive meditation.

I've only recently added it in to the daily practice, gradually going from the daily ritual only, to ritual plus divination, to all three practices per session. So it's very new, about a month along.

I have chosen the four sacred animals of the directions as the first subjects of the meditations, doing about three sessions each for Hawk of May, White Stag, Salmon of Wisdom, and Great Bear. Meditation on the hawk brought some really good stuff, some very useful information.

The meditation on the Stag (I have done one so far) was good but felt more like a skrying session, with more visual imagery and a sense of presence in my space, literally standing at my right shoulder. I still was able to follow thought streams pretty well, and ponder what was revealed so I figured it was within acceptable tolerances of practice variation. The thing that confused me a bit was that the stag came with a name, and that name was in Greek! Not Welsh. And with that name the Stag's whiteness took on a very slight golden yellow tint, away from the pure brilliant whiteness I saw -just- before. These were not too alarming, but presented a couple of red flags to ask about later.

I am wondering if the name coming to me in Greek might be a residual effect from my previous stretch of intensive devotional practice (and current casual honoring), which happened to be with Greek beings. That that is the Stag's name for me, it just got put through a Greek filter (does that happen?). Or if this is someone else showing up who isn't really the White Stag, and needs to be gently shown the door.

I thought perhaps I should try using the Welsh equivalent of the meaning of the name and see if it responds to my calling it by that name. Or English, for that matter. But this gets more into evocation territory, and I'm supposed to be meditating!

I'll see what happens in today's session, doing it just like I did last time.

I'm puzzled. Thoughts on the best course of action?
Thanks!
Bonnie

replies.title: Re: Which Stag?
replies.content: This kind of thing happens all the time. Write down what you got, use it as a source of themes for meditation, and proceed from there.

replies.title: Re: Which Stag?
replies.content: Thank you, will do!

replies.title: Re: Uptaking the Tarot
replies.content: Different people find different approaches worthwhile. My wife always writes things down in her current Tarot notebook; I never do; we're about equally good with the deck. Experiment, and figure out what works best for you.

replies.title: Re: Celtic Golden Dawn, (Lesser) Summoning Ritual of the Pentagram
replies.content: Go back and reread it again. Then pick up the book and walk through the instructions as though you were doing the ritual, doing exactly what the instructions ask you to do, and you'll figure it out. The ritual is written the way it is for a reason; you might meditate on what that reason might be.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
You've mentioned before that in your previous incarnation you were a woman who was killed in a car crash. Was she into esotericism? Is there a clear thread from what she experienced to what you are experiencing and achieving in this life?

Thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
She spent most of her relatively short life (she died before her fortieth birthday) running away from esotericism, for complex reasons. It was the life before that (an English businessman and Freemason, heavily involved in certain corners of the London occult scene) and another life further back (another Englishman of roughly Regency date, a Cambridge scholar with a passion for the old Neoplatonists) that were instrumental in putting me on the Path, and it was in another life still further back, at the time of the Thirty Years War, that an encounter with the literature of the Rosicrucian movement shook me out of some very dark places and brought about the moment of decision that sets every soul on the way to the Mysteries.

That said, yes, there are a lot of common factors linking this life with the previous one -- and it's in no way an accident that the occultism that's always worked best for me, and taught me the most, is the stuff that was flourishing in her lifetime, the stuff she should have studied and practiced. I've long thought that if she'd done that, she wouldn't have been in that car crash.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Would you mind explaining why she was running from it? Also, running from it seems to imply that she was heavily exposed but chose to ignore it all, is that correct?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I'm going to duck the first part of that, as it ties into certain issues I'd rather not discuss here. As for the second, no, not at all. Have you never encountered someone who took a sudden intense dislike to something, for no apparent reason at all? Very often, when that happens, there are issues from previous lives involved.

replies.title: This Is Interesting
replies.content:
Hi John,

How can you tell the difference between a real and unreal past life? During the last century the British Society for Psychical Research found, to their surprise, that a number of people who seemed to be remembering a past life were actually regurgitating a long-forgotten historical novel or movie. These people weren’t trying to con anyone; they honestly remembered past lives and had honestly forgotten the source material. Seems like that could happen to anybody who reads a lot.

Me, I’m doing well to remember last week, much less last millennium, so it probably won’t happen to me, but I’m curious as to how people resolve this potential knot.—Fuzzy

replies.title: Re: This Is Interesting
replies.content: You always have to watch out for cryptomnesia -- the technical term for this kind of forgotten memory -- and also for straightforward confabulation. If you go to the literature on past life memory research, which is considerable, you'll find methods for sorting those out discussed in quite some detail.

comments.title: Inflame thyself with prayer
comments.content:

Kevin

replies.title: Re: Inflame thyself with prayer
replies.content: In that case, you'll get the failure that you expect. I don't recommend it.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I hope this isn't an inappropriate question to ask, but I'll leave that up to you to decide.

Raising earth energy seems natural enough, but it regularly results in, ahem, as a man, something also rising. That's all fine and dandy, but it's not exactly what I'm looking for and it is quite a distraction. I appreciate that this energy is of a sexual nature and I can imagine that I probably have various (unexamined) sexual hangups, but how do I move on from here? How do I raise the energy to the heart, say? Do I somehow first have to clear out these blockages, these hangups, or is this just normal?

I don't know if I have explained myself clearly enough, but thanks in advance for any help with this!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
This is quite normal, and not just for men; I've had female students who had certain issues with moisture, shall we say, in the same situation. Magical energies are also sexual energies -- not surprisingly, as we're talking about the life force again.

What specific practice are you doing? There are ways to balance things out so that the energy doesn't get stuck in the sexual center, but they vary somewhat depending on what you're doing to raise the earth energy.

comments.title: Sphere of Protection Elements
comments.content:
I'm starting the system given in The Druid Magic Handbook but with Norse deities and symbolism. It seems to fit together rather well I think, similarities of European myths I suppose...

One aspect I'm wondering about is the elements. In Norse mythology there's a lot of symbolism of Ice and Fire, (origin of the cosmos and all that).

My question is what effect swapping EARTH for ICE in the north would have on the sphere of protection?

On the one hand Its 'Druid Magic', where EARTH seems like too important of a component to remove(my thinking is that EARTH would be the manifestation of all these things, but that may be irrelevant here).

On the other hand ICE is also Cold, Dry, a Solid, and with its prominence in Norse symbolism it seems like something I should include...

So, do you know off the bat if that swap would mess up this system of magic, or is this a 'give it a try and see what happens' sort of situation?

replies.title: Re: Sphere of Protection Elements
replies.content:
Don't tinker with a system you haven't yet practiced well enough to understand. It's like deciding to put a cup of salt into a cookie recipe in place of a cup of sugar -- hey, it's just another white powder, right? The results with magic are just as unpalatable.

The Sphere of Protection and the rest of the work in The Druid Magic Handbook can be adapted by changing out one pantheon for another (although you should be careful to keep the gender balance in place -- using all gods or all goddesses tends to produce unhelpful imbalances) but the elemental structure is hardwired into it, and should not be changed.

comments.title: Professions governed by Mercury
comments.content:
From Goats and Roses

I used the geomancy oracle as you instructed in the book to find out who is the ruler of my guardian genius, which turned out to be Mercury. However the chart did not perfect, so I am not living in harmony with my guardian genius.

I am considering a change of occupation (for lots of reasons) and would like to know what professions are ruled by Mercury. Would entering one of those fields bring me more in line with my purpose?

replies.title: Re: Professions governed by Mercury
replies.content: Mercury rules any profession that relies on language -- writing, publishing, journalism, public speaking, teaching. It also governs buying and selling, as well as theft, fraud, and trickery. As for your specific question, why not cast a chart about that?

replies.title: Re: Professions governed by Mercury
replies.content: Did I miss that part of the geomancy book? Or is it not in every edition?

replies.title: Re: Professions governed by Mercury
replies.content: It's in my book The Art and Practice of Geomancy, not in my other two books on geomancy.

comments.title: Astrological chart of the U.S.
comments.content:
From Goats and Roses

A while back you did a post, which I cannot find now, where you discussed the astrological chart of the U.S.A. You mentioned that there was something in the chart that was also shared by JFK and one other person. This chart relationship corresponded with an abrupt and catastrophic loss of power (and in JFK's case, death.)

What is the implication for the U.S.A.? It seems like the recent news of Russia's new weaponry, against which we have no defense, abruptly ended our pretensions to be the top world power. What in your opinion are the implications of America's birth chart that you highlighted?

replies.title: Re: Astrological chart of the U.S.
replies.content:
You're talking about Saturn in the tenth house. Yep; JFK had it, and so did Hitler and the German emperor Wilhelm II. It's considered a very strong indication that the person (or other entity) whose natal or foundation chart it is will rise to great power and then suffer a catastrophic fall from which there will be no recovery. The United States foundation chart I use, the one with the ascendant at 13 degrees Sagittarius, has Saturn in that position.

That said, it's very hard to know in advance when that potentiality in the foundation chart will be activated. I'll discuss that when I set out the ingress chart in a couple of weeks.

comments.title: Discursive vs. Mind-Emptying
comments.content:
Hello JMG

Why do you so strongly prefer discursive meditation over the various mind-emptying types?

SMJ

replies.title: Re: Discursive vs. Mind-Emptying
replies.content:
For two reasons. First of all, the mind-emptying meditations in their traditional contexts are fine, because practitioners of (say) Theravadin Buddhism combine sessions of meditation with time spent studying the sutras and doing other things to work with their minds. Mind-emptying meditation in the western world, though, has by and large been stripped of these balancing practices, and simply turns into a non-chemical tranquilizer. That's why so many big corporations are encouraging their employees to practice it; it helps keep the employees calm, smiling, harmless, and obedient.

Second, if you're going to practice a western occult path -- and that is, after all, what I practice and teach -- discursive meditation is an essential part of those paths. All those ornate emblems, from tarot trumps to alchemical diagrams? They're meant to be unpacked in discursive meditation. Practices like pathworking and scrying in the spirit vision? More than half the benefits come from exploring the results in discursive meditation. The traditional texts of those paths? They contain all kinds of hints and clues that you'll only catch if you explore them in discursive meditation. Practicing occultism without discursive meditation is like tying on a blindfold before you go to an art gallery.

comments.title: SOP working
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
I'm considering an SOP-based working to help me become a more effective speaker, and I had a few questions about it.

For the banishing portion of the working, I'm torn between banishing the fears of judgment and vulnerability that I believe are at the root of my problems vocalizing my thoughts, and banishing the specific symptoms of my difficulty - the mumbling, the hesitations, and so on.
Seeing as how "fear of judgment" is a big, nebulous topic, I worry about turning myself into a raging blowhard if I'm not careful about my working.
Any advice?

Also, if I use the Grove Ceremony version of the working, would I just do it once, rather than the seven times you recommend for the SOP version?

Thanks,
Cliff

replies.title: Re: SOP working
replies.content:
Why not simply focus on invoking success as a speaker, and banishing all internal barriers to that success?

The grove version also does it seven times, once for each element -- air, fire, water, earth, spirit below, spirit above, and spirit within. That's important for balance.

replies.title: Re: SOP working
replies.content:
Hello Anonymous,

First the working and then the work, as the adage has it. I've gotten over many of my fears about public speaking (and I had quite a few, even after almost two decades of middle-school teaching) through Toastmasters. Learning to practice a system of public speaking is a magic all its own; and Toastmasters has the advantage of being a lodge-like system of exoteric study.

replies.title: Re: SOP working
replies.content:
Hi--I can second the Toastmaster suggestion, with the caveat that it's been several decades since I did the program.
Two more things that helped this profound introvert with a very quiet voice:
Increasing my body's contentnent with itself via (probably the endorphins) of long walks, muscle work, and working with my scoliosis affected posture.
Posture is a big deal with opera singers, for example.

The other mental trick:
the situation for your speech is akin to an actor on a stage.
The performance aspect can get you to focus on your audience; when you do that, you forget about your anxiety.
Breathe.
Look slightly above your audience's eyes; eventually, you'll feel comfortable to look at individual audience members.
If in a room, pitch your voice into the corners of the room.

Not least, remember: your audience truly wants you to do well (why else would they have come to listen?

Finally, if there's someone whose speaking you admire, why not "try them on for size" --see what you can learn from that voice, its pacing, and the silences.

And, breathe.

comments.title: Healing and imagination
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

A little while ago you told me to keep practising the LRP in my minds eye as my shoulder was broken and I can't actually lift my right arm (it's still pretty bad) I've since come across a man called Joe Dispenza who apparently healed a broken back with his imagination and intention alone. Would you call that magic?

Any sources you would recommend for helping my healing that way?

Best wishes

replies.title: Re: Healing and imagination
replies.content: You'd probably be better off learning from Mr. Dispenza. It's not something I've studied or practiced.

comments.title: The Order of the Athanor
comments.content:
Every few years, I find myself pulling INSIDE A MAGICAL LODGE down from a shelf to explain some concept from it to someone; and almost every time I find myself reading or re-reading long sections of it.

And today, I'm wondering "Why not?" Do you think the Order of the Athanor, as currently written/presented, is a workable lodge on its own? That is, could a magical group practice it with effective results, develop inner plane contacts, and launch it as a live magical option?

Reading it again, I alternate between "yes, of course!" and "hmmm, not sure." One of its weaknesses has always seemed to be the lack of an education or training program — and yet it must be admitted that buried in the Golden Dawn education program somewhere is the etheric programming of its own self-destruction in egomania and factional rivalries. There's obviously an important balance to be struck somewhere between a "minimum / sufficient symbol-set" and "an overblown symbol-set that creates personality and egregore defects" in any lodge group — and the Order of the Athanor seems like it's somewhere close to the "minimum/sufficient symbol-set" line, maybe even below.

Just as the proof of the pudding is in the eating, though — I expect that the proof of a lodge's capacity to be successful, magical, make inner plane contacts and transform its members, is in the living...

Still, to restate my question, which may have gotten lost in my own ponderings... Did you intend for the Order of the Athanor to be a completely workable system, or are there certain essentials (like a study/education program) that are effectively missing from the Order, and would need to be added to make it workable?

replies.title: Re: The Order of the Athanor
replies.content: I wrote the thing without intending anybody to use the Order of the Athanor system, but field tested all the rituals and made sure they worked. I've heard from several people in various places who've used them also, to good effect, so it's potentially an option. As for an education program, funny -- do you recall the discussion of that in the fictionalized account of the work?

replies.title: Re: The Order of the Athanor
replies.content:
Dear John Michael,

Of course I do! And the appointment of a committee to decide in one particular lodge what the core education program should be, quite separately from the structure of opening, closing and initiation rites... Hmm!

I've got a group of people locally that are reading Iamblichus, Taylor's Orphic Hymns, Porphyry, Plato and other Platonic and Neo-platonic sources. Maybe we could vote to institute an Athanor-style group as our working process, and keep our reading program. ... Hmmm.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Do you think the ascendance of Christianity in Europe diminished the effectiveness of the old pagan magic, and is the modern lack of interest in Christianity causing pagan magic to once again surge in power?

Or, does magic have little to do with religion and more to do with the individual practitioner?

Thank you.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's an exceedingly complex question. My basic answer to it unfolds from the points made here.

comments.title: Re: Book reports
comments.content: Ronald's books are interesting but written from within a very specific and highly politicized viewpoint; I don't find them useful as guides to Druid Revival practice and philosophy, which is what The Druidry Handbook is all about. Lewis Spence, on the other hand, was one of the most learned occultists of his day, a really first-rate scholar as well as an operative mage, and I recommend all his books highly as guides to Druid Revival philosophy and (in veiled form) practice.

comments.title: prayer vs. magic
comments.content:
Can you expound a little bit on the differences between magic and prayer?

It seems (to me at least) that prayer is a wider category than magic: something, perhaps, like conversation with discarnate spiritual beings. (Leave aside the archaic English usages such as "pray tell" spoken to our fellow incarnate humans.) So, prayer will include getting-to-know-you, various sorts of conversation for establishing or maintaining a relationship, simply connecting with another being out of love, or for its own sake, etc. But some prayer also asks the being to whom we pray for some concrete result... and now we shade into the comparison/contrast with magic.

Some specific queries, though I'd welcome your thoughts on the wider comparative question to whatever extent you're willing to share them:

(1) Clearly, what I've already written would benefit from a solid typology of types of prayer, written from a magically-literate perspective. Can you (or anyone else here) recommend such a thing already in print somewhere?

(2) What are the ethics of praying for a third party? You've discussed elsewhere some of the good reasons why one should never do a magical working for/on a third party without their consent; do any related concerns apply to prayer? It's quite common in many religious traditions to pray, for instance, that a god heal a sick person without asking the sick person first.

(2a) It seems that there is an interesting contrast between the case of praying for someone's health (the end goal of which, at least in general terms, they may often be presumed to want) and praying for someone's conversion--which monotheists are notorious for, but which almost by definition seems to entail a violation of the intended convert's free will. (Yes, the discussion of love magic from the other blog is on my mind here.) But perhaps there's a similar distinction between different sorts of magical workings?

(3) You've discussed recently how a magical working lays down indelible "tracks in space" which in some important sense we cannot erase. Is there anything similar in the case of petitionary prayer? Or is the door open to saying, in a later prayer, "oops, sorry, that was a terrible request, never should have asked, please don't do that, I apologize profusely, etc." and have that be the end of it?

Thanks for your time and consideration in hosting this forum, and giving the community so many thoughtful answers!

--barefootwisdom

replies.title: Re: prayer vs. magic
replies.content: That's enough material for an entire post, which I'll consider writing at some point in the future.

comments.title: Examining past life’s memories
comments.content: I’ve had several flashes of “insights” when contemplating past lives, and I was wondering what are some ways to check against these flashes for validity?

replies.title: Re: Examining past life’s memories
replies.content: Write them down and then let go of them. See what surfaces later on. It's by the slow accumulation of such glimpses, fostered by regular meditation, that you can get a sense of the shape of your previous lives.
